lesson.menu_title: Handling Objections -- Answering Doubts & Saying No Politely
lesson.subtitle: Aula 20 -- Handling Objections -- Answering Doubts & Saying No Politely
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rafael de Andrade Brandão | Aula 20 -- Handling Objections

listenings.text: Hi Rafael, this is Diana. Great pitch last week. Now, here is the next skill: handling objections. An objection is when the client has a doubt or says no. Do not panic. Every good salesperson hears objections, so let me share three steps. First, never argue. When the client raises a doubt, do not say they are wrong. Instead, reassure them. Show that you understand. You can say, I understand your concern, or that is a fair point. Second, answer the doubt, but stay polite. If the client says the price is too high, do not just say no. Soften it. Say, I am afraid we cannot lower the price, but I can offer a longer guarantee. Always give a but, never a flat no. Third, sometimes you must decline a request, and that is fine. Just be polite and flexible. Say, unfortunately we cannot do that, however we can be flexible on the start date. And remember, if a client hesitates, it does not mean no. It means they want more information. So stay calm, reassure them, and turn every drawback into a reason to trust you. You handled the pitch beautifully. Now go and handle the objections. Good luck, Rafael.

Okay, let me practice handling the client's objections before the meeting. So, the client will probably say the price is high. Here is my plan. First, I reassure them. I will say, I understand your concern, and that is a fair point. I will never tell them they are wrong. Then, I answer the doubt, but I stay polite. I am afraid we cannot lower the price, but I can offer a longer guarantee and free support for one year. Notice, I never give a flat no. I always add a but. Next, if they ask for something I cannot do, I will decline politely. Unfortunately we cannot deliver in one week, however we can be flexible and start the project early. And if the client hesitates, I will not push. A doubt is not a no. It only means they want more information, so I will reassure them again. Every drawback is a chance to build trust. Okay, that felt calm and professional. I reassured the client, I softened every no, and I stayed flexible. Diana will be proud.

extra_audio.text: A client raises an objection about the price. First, Rafael reassures the client and says he understands the concern. Then he answers the doubt but stays polite and never gives a flat no. Next, he softens the answer with a but and offers a longer guarantee instead. After that, he politely declines a request he cannot meet, but stays flexible on the start date. Finally, he reassures the client one more time, because a doubt is not a no, it only means they want more information.
